sources.name: Super Metroid Wiki — Horizontal Speed
sources.notes: Primary source for horizontal movement constants. Walk max 2.49152 px/f, speed booster max 7.0 px/f, acceleration 0.0625 px/f², ramp frames. High confidence.

sources.name: TASVideos Game Resources — SNES / Super Metroid
sources.notes: Technique documentation: mockball, walljump, bomb jumping, Shinespark. Vertical physics primarily qualitative in available resources.

sources.name: Data Crystal — Super Metroid
sources.notes: RAM map for physics variable addresses. Supplements speedrun wiki.

notes: Horizontal movement constants (walk max, speed booster, acceleration) are high-confidence from wiki.supermetroid.run.
Vertical physics (jump initial velocity, gravity) are medium confidence — community-measured, ROM address not confirmed in reviewed sources.
Super Metroid uses a 16-bit sub-pixel precision system. Exact sub-pixel unit per pixel: acceleration documented as '4096 sub-units' suggesting 65536 sub-pixels/pixel (16.16 fixed-point) or similar.
Super Metroid is the SNES sequel to NES Metroid (1986). Major additions: wall jump, Space Jump, Grapple Beam, Speed Booster, Charge Beam, Shinespark.
Level structure inherited from NES Metroid: room-based, not continuous scroll. Rooms are larger than one screen (smooth scroll within room).
NTSC (60fps) values used throughout.

scrolling_architecture.description: Rooms are larger than one screen. Camera scrolls smoothly within a room following Samus. Transitioning through a door triggers a room load and screen transition. Architecturally similar to NES Metroid's room graph, but rooms are multi-screen-sized.

walk_max_speed.notes: Maximum walking speed — standard horizontal movement cap without Speed Booster. Samus has acceleration to reach this, unlike the instant-velocity Mega Man.

acceleration.notes: 4096 sub-units of acceleration per frame. If using 65536 sub-pixels/pixel: 4096/65536 = 0.0625 px/frame². Ramp from standing to walk max takes ~32 frames.

ramp_frames_from_standing.notes: Samus must run continuously for ~112 frames to reach Speed Booster max. This creates a significant activation run-up requirement.

notes: Speed Booster is a pickup upgrade — not available at game start.
At max Speed Booster velocity (7.0 px/frame), Samus destroys Speed Boost blocks on contact.
Speed Booster speed is 7.0 / 2.49152 = 2.81x normal walk max — nearly triple standard speed.
The 112-frame ramp requires approximately 1.87 seconds of continuous running — level design must create long-enough corridors for activation.

shinespark.description: After reaching Speed Booster max speed, crouching stores the Shinespark charge. Pressing jump releases it: Samus moves in a locked cardinal or diagonal direction at Speed Booster max velocity.
notes: Shinespark allows boosting through solid blocks in a fixed trajectory.
The locked-direction movement after release makes Shinespark a skill-expression tool: players must pre-aim the spark before activating.
Chains of Shinesparks can be set up for sequence breaks, reaching areas well before intended progression.
Shinespark is not a base ability — requires Speed Booster upgrade first.

initial_y_velocity_approx.notes: Approximate from community documentation and gameplay measurement. Exact ROM value not confirmed in sources reviewed. VERIFY.

notes: Variable height — holding jump longer produces higher apex.
Samus's jump feel is lighter than NES Metroid's floaty jump — gravity is higher in Super Metroid.
Spin jump (pressing jump while pressing left or right) initiates a spinning animation that interacts with wall jump and Space Jump.

spin_jump.description: Jumping while moving left or right initiates a spinning Samus animation. Required for wall jump and Space Jump activation.
spin_jump.notes: Spin jump can also deflect certain projectiles.

gravity.notes: Approximate. Super Metroid gravity is higher than NES Metroid (~0.25 px/f² NES) but still on the lighter end for SNES. VERIFY against ROM.

notes: Wall jump is always available in Super Metroid — NOT a pickup upgrade. Absent in NES Metroid entirely.
Technique: spin jump toward a wall, press jump at moment of wall contact while holding away from the wall.
Wall jump is one of the highest-skill mechanics — timing window is tight but learnable.
Used in advanced speedruns to access areas without required mobility items (sequence breaks).
Multiple wall kicks can chain to climb tall shafts.
Space Jump allows infinite mid-air jumps when timed correctly during the spin jump animation.
Timing: pressing jump at the lowest point of the spin animation cycle extends the jump.
Space Jump must be obtained as a pickup in Maridia.
Not present in NES Metroid — introduced in Super Metroid.

morph_ball.notes: Morph ball speed is approximately equal to walk max speed. Allows passage through 1-tile-height tunnels.
morph_ball.input: Down to morph, Up to unmorph (or press Up + jump for Spring Ball jump if equipped)

hitbox.notes: 1 tile × 1 tile. Fits through corridors inaccessible to standing Samus.

spring_ball.notes: Spring Ball upgrade allows jumping while in morph ball form (Up + Jump). Without it, Samus must unmorph to jump.

notes: Grapple Beam latches onto designated ceiling tiles.
Physics: Samus swings in a pendulum arc from the grapple point, gaining momentum.
Releasing at the swing apex sends Samus in the direction of travel.
Not in NES Metroid — introduced in Super Metroid.

charge_beam.notes: Charge Beam allows charging the arm cannon for a more powerful shot. Not in NES Metroid — introduced here. Can be combined with beam upgrades (Ice, Wave, Spazer, Plasma).

samus_standing.notes: Approximate. VERIFY against ROM. Samus's hitbox is narrower than her sprite.

absent_from_nes_metroid.notes: All mechanics listed above are Super Metroid additions. NES Metroid only has: standard jump, morph ball, bombs, missile, ice/wave/long beam variations.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Horizontal acceleration (0.0625 px/frame², 32 frames to max) gives Super Metroid a fluid momentum feel between the instant-velocity Mega Man and the fast-accelerating Mario. It's fast enough to not feel sluggish but slow enough that reversing direction has a 'weight.'

The Speed Booster + Shinespark system creates a second movement layer: normal exploration (up to 2.49 px/f) vs. high-speed layer (7 px/f). Good Metroid level design hides secrets behind speed booster blocks that casual players may never notice.
Wall jump and Space Jump are skill-gated, not item-gated — sequence-breaking through skill mastery is a core Super Metroid design value. The game wants expert players to break it.

Room-based level generation should model a room graph where rooms have multiple connection points (doors at different heights/positions). The graph should have choke points gated by upgrades but also bypass routes gated by skill.

The gradient from walk → speed booster should manifest in level design: long corridors that look like simple hallways reward the player with activation of the high-speed movement mode, then immediately test the player's ability to use it.
